Title: BYPASS1: How a Tiny Mutant Tells a Big Story about Root-to-shoot Signaling

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: BPS1 prevents overproduction of a root-derived mobile signal; loss of BPS1 causes shoot growth arrest, abnormal root development, and reduced auxin responsiveness in shoots
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. bps1 mutant roots are necessary and sufficient to arrest shoot development, as shown by grafting and root excision. 2. BPS1 acts negatively to prevent synthesis or xylem entry of a root-derived mobile signal. 3. Signal production requires carotenoid biosynthesis but is neither ABA nor strigolactone. 4. The mobile signal inhibits auxin-responsive transcription in the shoot.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: map-based cloning; mutant phenotyping; grafting; root excision; chemical inhibitor rescue; double-mutant analysis; DR5:GUS auxin reporter assays
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: plant-specific 249 aa protein with a single large uncharacterized domain
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: long-distance root-to-shoot signaling that modifies shoot growth and root development
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: root-derived, moves through xylem to shoot
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: grafting; root excision; carotenoid biosynthesis inhibitor treatment; exogenous 2,4-D in reporter assays
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: loss of function causes severe pleiotropic defects including arrested shoot growth, radialized leaves, short roots, and abnormal differentiation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to manipulate root-to-shoot signaling and water-use related traits, but severe pleiotropy and unknown signal chemistry require caution
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review states current model suggests BPS1 is required to prevent synthesis of a novel substance that moves from root to shoot and interferes with auxin signaling; not ABA or strigolactone.
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: DR5 synthetic auxin-responsive promoter (reporter, inducible by auxin)

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: putative carotenoid-dependent mobile signal analogous to the Arabidopsis BPS1 pathway
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing NbBPS1 causes abnormal leaf development and ectopic cambium proliferation; phenotypes are partially suppressed by fluridone, supporting negative regulation of a carotenoid-dependent mobile signal
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NbBPS1 is a BPS-family homolog functionally analyzed in tobacco by VIGS. 2. NbBPS1 silencing induced abnormal leaf development and ectopic cambium proliferation. 3. Fluridone partially suppressed the silencing phenotype, supporting conservation of carotenoid-dependent BPS signaling. 4. Silenced plants also showed increased leaf ploidy, implicating BPS genes in normal cell cycle progression.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: virus-induced gene silencing; phenotype analysis; fluridone suppression
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: BPS family protein; predicted Arabidopsis BPS2 homolog
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: long-distance developmental signaling affecting shoot development
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: silencing causes abnormal development and altered ploidy
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Supports functional conservation of BPS-family signaling in crops; manipulation could affect development broadly and must be tightly controlled
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Tobacco VIGS system was not thought to silence roots, suggesting tissue-specific specialization within the BPS gene family.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: putative carotenoid-dependent mobile signal analogous to the Arabidopsis BPS1 pathway
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Identified as a BPS-family homolog in Nicotiana benthamiana, but direct metabolism-related functional evidence in this review is limited
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NbBPS2 was isolated as a Nicotiana benthamiana BPS homolog. 2. Both NbBPS1 and NbBPS2 were predicted to be Arabidopsis BPS2 homologs. 3. Direct functional evidence summarized here centers on NbBPS1, not NbBPS2.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: BPS family protein; predicted Arabidopsis BPS2 homolog
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Currently limited without direct functional validation
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as a homolog; not a primary experimentally validated core metabolism gene in the review, but included among summarized BPS family members.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: core carotenoid pathway upstream of cyclization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: desaturates phytoene toward downstream colored carotenoid intermediates required for carotenoid formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activity is required for synthesis of the BPS1-dependent mobile signal; inhibition partially rescues bps1 shoot and root defects
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Fluridone inhibition of phytoene desaturase partially rescued bps1 shoot and root phenotypes. 2. This indicates the mobile bps1 signal requires carotenoid biosynthesis. 3. The result supports that the signal is a novel carotenoid derivative rather than ABA or strigolactone.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: chemical inhibition with fluridone; phenotypic rescue analysis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: growth on fluridone-supplemented medium under low light
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: root-to-shoot long-distance signaling that arrests shoot growth and alters root development in bps1
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: carotenoid biosynthesis inhibition causes bleaching and small plant size in wild type
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited direct value because inhibition broadly disrupts carotenoids; useful mainly for pathway dissection of the unknown mobile signal
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Gene itself was not a primary experimental subject; enzyme step was implicated through inhibitor studies only. Included because carotenoid-pathway steps were directly used to infer signal origin.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: cyclizes lycopene to cyclic carotenoids in the carotenoid pathway
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activity is required for synthesis of the BPS1-dependent mobile signal; inhibition partially rescues bps1 phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CPTA inhibition of lycopene cyclase partially rescued bps1 shoot and root defects. 2. The BPS1-dependent mobile signal requires carotenoid biosynthetic steps through lycopene cyclization. 3. Data support a novel carotenoid-derived signal distinct from ABA and strigolactone.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: chemical inhibition with CPTA; phenotypic rescue analysis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: growth on CPTA-supplemented medium under low light
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: root-to-shoot long-distance signaling that arrests shoot growth and alters root development in bps1
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: CPTA treatment broadly perturbs carotenoid metabolism and causes bleached, small wild-type plants
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for mechanistic dissection but not straightforward for crop engineering due to broad carotenoid disruption
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Gene itself was not a focal cloned gene; inference is based on inhibitor-defined enzymatic step.
